name: Pivot Grotesk
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque by the foundry, drawing from early grotesque traditions while blending radial and logarithmic spiral forms, resulting in a distinctive, energetic take on the genre.
summary: A grotesque by Anton Studer that blends the irregularity of early grotesques with forms derived from radial timekeeping lines and logarithmic spirals, originally cut for Theater Basel in 2020.
description: Designed by Anton Studer and originally commissioned by Nouvelle Noire for Theater Basel in 2020, Pivot Grotesk arrived as a retail release in 2024 after refinement and expansion to sixteen styles across eight weights. Its letterforms draw on two visual sources: the radial lines of clocks and movement diagrams, and the logarithmic spirals seen in natural and astronomical phenomena. The result sits outside rational geometric sans territory, carrying an organic energy that makes it suited to cultural institutions and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Pivot Grotesk as drawing inspiration from radial lines found in timekeeping and locomotion visualisations, alongside the logarithmic spirals of nature and the Milky Way. Blending these principles with the unrestrained style of early grotesques produced a typeface that moves beyond rational geometry. Originally designed for Theater Basel in 2020, it was refined and expanded for its 2024 retail release.

name: Arosa
classification_notes: Walthert describes NN Arosa as a Swiss constructed humanist sans serif, drawing from the hand-drawn lettering of early 20th-century Swiss tourism poster artists rather than calligraphic tradition.
summary: A humanist sans-serif by Edgar Walthert that reinterprets the bold, hand-drawn lettering of early 20th-century Swiss tourism posters into a contemporary text family.
description: Rather than reviving a specific historical face, Walthert drew from the pragmatic expressiveness of poster artists such as Hugo Laubi, Karl Bickel and Carl Böckli, distilling their energy into a constructed humanist sans with nine weights and matching italics. The text version prioritises legibility, with small caps given particular attention as a distinguishing typographic feature. A display and script variant are planned to complete the super family in later releases.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe NN Arosa as a fresh interpretation of Swiss graphic heritage rather than a strict revival, inspired by vintage Swiss tourism poster lettering and originally commissioned by a Swiss client. Designed by Edgar Walthert and released in 2024, it is conceived as the text branch of a super family that will eventually include display and script versions, with broad coverage of central and east European languages.

name: Editeur
classification_notes: Rooted in IBM Golfball typewriter letterforms, the family carries the distinctive fixed-pitch aesthetic of analog machinery across three subfamilies — Italic, Serif and Semi — placing it firmly in the typewriter-derived serif tradition.
summary: A three-part type family derived from an IBM Golfball typewriter specimen, spanning italic, serif and semi cuts across five weights each.
description: Originally sparked by a single Light Italic from an IBM Selectric specimen, the family was developed by Alexander Colby and subsequently overhauled and expanded by Timo Gaessner into a full release. Three distinct subfamilies — Éditeur Italic, Éditeur Serif and Éditeur Semi — each run through Thin to Bold, giving the family considerable range for editorial and screen use. The typewriter origins are worn openly rather than concealed, lending the forms a considered analog warmth.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Editeur as beginning with a spark from the past — specifically the Light Italic found on an IBM Golfball typewriter in an old IBM Selectric specimen. What started as a one-weight digital revival evolved into a three-part family encompassing Éditeur Italic, Éditeur Serif and Éditeur Semi, each available in five weights, framed as a quiet homage to analog machinery reimagined for the screen and page.

name: Konrad
classification_notes: Konrad is rooted in the proto-roman typefaces of 1465, the earliest printed roman letterforms, blending incunabula humanist serif characteristics with residual gothic script elements into a contemporary interpretation.
summary: A contemporary interpretation of the earliest printed roman typefaces, drawn by Martina Meier with gothic script traces folded into a clean, modern structure.
description: Martina Meier began researching early roman type specimens at the Zurich University of the Arts in 2015, tracing the letterforms printed by Konrad Sweynheym and Arnold Pannartz at Subiaco in 1465. The result is a three-weight family — Regular, Medium and Bold — that carries archetypical gothic script elements within an otherwise modern and restrained roman. The character set is broad, extending into obscure and uncommon signs that reflect the typeface's scholarly origins.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Konrad as a contemporary take on the first printed roman typeface, designed by Martina Meier. Rooted in specimens discovered at the Zurich University of the Arts library, the family covers three weights and embeds gothic script elements into a clean contemporary roman, accompanied by a dedicated microsite retracing the journey of its two historical namesakes, Konrad Sweynheym and Arnold Pannartz.

name: Nouvelle Grotesk
classification_notes: A Swiss grotesque in the tradition of Akzidenz Grotesk and Helvetica, with the foundry explicitly positioning it within the Swiss Style Grotesque lineage. The 'Diagonal' cuts replace a conventional italic with a sloped roman.
summary: A Swiss grotesque by Anton Studer that positions itself within the Helvetica and Akzidenz lineage, offering seven weights each paired with a sloped 'Diagonal' companion rather than a conventional italic.
description: Released in 2020, the family runs from Thin to Fat across fourteen styles, with each upright weight matched by a Diagonal cut that substitutes for a traditional italic. Nouvelle Noire frame it as a contribution to the Swiss grotesque canon alongside Helvetica, Univers and Akkurat, aiming for simplicity with a degree of warmth rather than pure neutrality. The character set covers extended Latin with accented and special characters, making it a workable choice for multilingual European work.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire trace the grotesque tradition from Akzidenz Grotesk through Miedinger's Neue Haas Grotesk and Frutiger's Univers, and position Nouvelle Grotesk as a contemporary contribution to that Swiss lineage. Designed by Anton Studer and released in 2020, they describe the typeface as sitting between simplicity and neutrality, with forms intended to encourage creativity and retain a trace of the original, slightly eccentric spirit of the word 'grotesque'.

name: Swinton
classification_notes: Swinton draws directly from Swiss grotesque tradition of the 1960s, characterised by low stroke contrast, with additional expressive details borrowed from American grotesque conventions.
summary: A grotesque by Marc Droz that fuses Swiss 1960s rationalism with expressive details drawn from American grotesque conventions, available in eleven weights with italics.
description: Released in 2023, Swinton traces its lineage to the low-stroke-contrast grotesques of the International Style while incorporating characterful details absent from its more austere predecessors. The family spans eleven weights from Extra Thin to Extra Black, each with a matching italic, giving it considerable range across editorial, branding and display contexts. Nouvelle Noire accompanied the release with a fictional narrative universe built using AI tools, framing the typeface within an invented cultural mythology.
foundry_blurb: Marc Droz designed Swinton as a versatile grotesque rooted in Swiss typefaces of the 1960s, known for low stroke contrast and association with the International Style, while introducing expressive details and the standardised spirit of American grotesques. To mark the blend of Swiss rigour and American ambition, Nouvelle Noire developed a fictional story for the typeface using ChatGPT and Stable Diffusion, centred on a fictional designer named Cram Zord who created Swinton to bring enchantment into everyday life.

name: Knit Grotesk
classification_notes: Knit Grotesk is a display typeface built on a strict horizontal line grid derived from slipstitch knitting patterns, giving it a bitmap-like, craft-oriented character that places it firmly in the display category rather than any conventional text genre.
summary: A display typeface by Rüdiger Schlömer designed specifically for hand knitting, built on the structural logic of slipstitch knitting patterns.
description: Originating from a low-resolution adaptation of Futura translated into knitting charts, the typeface developed its own typographic vocabulary through the constraints of the craft. Six cuts combine three weights with two background treatments: Dot, which uses a blank background, and Stripe, which applies a horizontal line grid derived from alternating two-tone double rows in slipstitch knitting. The cuts can be layered and combined to produce a wide range of typographic effects rooted in textile logic.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Knit Grotesk as a typeface by Rüdiger Schlömer made specifically for hand knitting, the outcome of his investigations into typographic knitting. Early characters were inspired by FuturX, a low-res adaptation of Futura by Laucke Siebein, which was then translated into a knitting pattern; from that process a full typeface emerged, with its characteristic horizontal line grid derived from slipstitch knitting. The six cuts result from three weights combined with two treatments, Stripe and Dot, developed with the support of Anton Studer.

name: Didot Modern
classification_notes: Explicitly rooted in the Didot and Didone tradition, with extreme stroke contrast, flat unbracketed serifs and vertical stress characteristic of neoclassical French printing types.
summary: A hybrid Didone by Arnaud Chemin that grafts geometric minimalism and grotesque-derived alternates onto the French neoclassical printing tradition.
description: Drawing from Nicolas Pierre Gando, Ludwig & Mayer's Didone and Jules Didot sources, Arnaud Chemin stripped out baroque flourishes and teardrop terminals in favour of flat endings and sharp triangular serifs that read as both historical and rigorously contemporary. Ten styles run across five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic. A broad stylistic set offers alternate characters for A, R, Q, V, W and others, constructed on grotesque principles that Chemin traces back through Clarendon to the Didot tradition itself. Suited to fashion, luxury, art publishing and corporate identity work where high contrast and precision at large sizes are required.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Didot Modern as a collaboration with Arnaud Chemin begun in 2019 and released in 2021. They position it as a hybrid typeface referencing the proportions of French Didone printing types, combined with expressive geometry and minimalism. Flat endings, sharp triangular serifs and a wide stylistic set — including grotesque-style alternates — bring the Didot legacy into a digital context where classicism meets rationalism.

name: Colroy
classification_notes: Colroy is explicitly described as a Clarendon-like slab serif, slightly more geometricised than traditional Clarendons but retaining their characteristic weight contrast and bracketed serifs.
summary: A contemporary Clarendon-style slab serif by Marc Droz, slightly geometricised relative to its historical forebears yet built primarily for body text legibility across 86 languages.
description: Designed by Marc Droz and originating from his studies at the Zurich University of the Arts in 2009, Colroy sits in the Swiss tradition of Clarendon revivals exemplified by Hermann Eidenbenz's 1950s redrawing for the Haas'sche Schriftgiesserei. The family runs across seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a companion italic, alongside Small Caps and a variable font added in the 2022 re-release. Its measured geometricisation keeps it versatile across body text and display settings without losing the rhythmic vividness that makes historical Clarendons readable at smaller sizes.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Colroy as a superbly crafted contemporary Clarendon-like typeface, notable for its suitability for body text, its refined italics and its support for up to 86 languages. They contextualise it within the Swiss tradition, drawing a line from Eidenbenz's 1950s redrawing of Clarendon to Marc Droz's modern interpretation. Originally completed in 2014 with six cuts, the family was re-released in 2022 with four additional weights and a variable font, accompanied by a dedicated microsite at colroy.ch.

name: Rektorat
classification_notes: Rektorat derives from 1933 institutional signage lettering, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with its even stroke weights and upright structure characteristic of early modernist European letterforms.
summary: A grotesque sans drawn from 1933 signage lettering discovered behind wallpaper during the 2001 renovation of the Zurich building housing the Kunstgewerbeschule and Museum für Gestaltung.
description: Rektorat traces its origins to institutional hand-lettering made for a Swiss modernist building in 1933, rediscovered decades later and painstakingly redrawn in ink by Rudolf Barmettler with students. The digital family, reworked from 2014 with Anton Studer, runs across 14 cuts in seven weights from Hairline to Heavy, each available in a standard and a Re variant. The result is a historically grounded grotesque with a specific provenance that gives it more character than a generic revival.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Rektorat as a type family rooted in 1933 lettering originally created for the signage of the modernist Zurich building that housed the Kunstgewerbeschule (now ZHdK) and the Museum für Gestaltung. The original letterings were found hidden behind wallpaper during a 2001 renovation, traced and redrawn in ink by Barmettler and students, then digitised. Since 2014 Barmettler and Studer have reworked every detail to produce a 14-cut family described as the first part of an ever-growing superfamily.

name: Alena
classification_notes: Based on Roman Capitals of the first and second century A.D. and humanistic minuscule letterforms, with optically constant line widths; firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A humanist serif in eight weights tracing its origins to a 1980 Jost Hochuli woodcut, redrawn with optically constant stroke widths for calm, unobtrusive text.
description: Roland Stieger drew Alena from a woodcut by Swiss typographer Jost Hochuli, anchoring the capitals in the proportions of Roman inscriptions from the first and second centuries AD and grounding the lowercase in the humanistic minuscule. Optically equalised stroke widths give the text a quiet evenness that holds at small sizes without appearing mechanical. The eight matching italics, each inclined at just 5 degrees with a narrow ductus recalling the earliest italic types, integrate smoothly into body text while remaining clearly distinct from the uprights.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Alena as originating from a 1980 Jost Hochuli woodcut, with capitals based on Roman proportions from the first and second century AD and lowercase drawn from late humanistic minuscule. Optical constant line widths give the text a calm, unobtrusive quality, and Stieger placed particular emphasis on legibility at small sizes. The italics carry a narrow ductus at a slight 5-degree inclination, recalling the economy of the earliest cut italic types, and the family is suited to complex typographic requirements as well as web use.

name: Allegra
classification_notes: Allegra draws its capital proportions from Roman Capitalis Monumentalis and its lowercase skeleton from early 16th-century Roman types, placing it firmly in the humanist serif tradition with a focus on legibility and restrained drawing.
summary: A humanist serif by Jost Hochuli with capitals rooted in Roman Capitalis Monumentalis and lowercase proportions drawn from early 16th-century Roman types, released in 14 weights with italics.
description: Designed by Jost Hochuli and released in July 2019, Allegra takes its capital counter shapes from first and early second century AD Roman monumental inscriptions, while the lowercase derives from the skeleton proportions of early Roman types from the first half of the 16th century. Three of its seven weights, Regular, Book and Medium, are deliberately close in colour, giving typographers fine control over text density within a single family. The range suits editorial work and large-scale signage systems equally well.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Allegra as a typeface whose name comes from the Rhaeto-Romance greeting used in the Swiss canton of Graubünden, meaning joy. They note that the word itself offers a useful preview of x-height proportions, ascenders and descenders, and that it contains the letter e, the most common in many European languages, as well as the tricky ra combination. The family spans 14 weights across Light, Regular, Book, Medium, Semibold, Bold and Black with matching italics, developed by Roland Stieger and Jonas Niedermann.

name: Noire
classification_notes: Though not strictly a historical blackletter revival, NN Noire pushes letterforms to the extreme edge of legibility using dense, brush-drawn strokes that place it firmly in the blackletter/ultra-black expressive category.
summary: A single-weight ultra-black display typeface drawn with ink and brush, designed to push letterforms to the very limit of legibility.
description: Drawn by Clovis Vallois with ink and brush on paper, NN Noire is a deliberate rejection of legibility and function in favour of raw graphic intensity. The forms are pushed to such extremes that the typeface reads more as texture or mark-making than conventional type. Released in 2018 alongside a limited letterpress poster by Swiss printer Dafi Kühne, it occupies a niche between experimental typography and fine-art printmaking.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe NN Noire as probably the blackest and most unreadable typeface out there, initially drawn with ink and brush on A4 paper. They frame it as an exploration beyond conventional type design, pushing existing letterforms to the edge of legibility with no ambitions toward conciseness or functionality, intended instead as a joyful creative exercise.

name: Ernesto
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a geometric sans serif, drawing on the spirit of Kabel and Futura from the 1920s; uppercase-only with a construction-based approach rooted in Bauhaus-era geometry.
summary: An uppercase-only geometric sans in three weights, originally designed for the corporate identity of a cultural venue in southern Germany, referencing the geometric type of the Weimar era.
description: Designed by Clovis Vallois and released between 2010 and 2011, Ernesto was commissioned for the Ewerk Freiburg cultural space, with its forms derived from the building's architecture. The typeface looks to the geometric sans serifs of the late 1920s, particularly Kabel and Futura, as its formal reference points. It exists only in uppercase across Thin, Regular and Bold weights, each accompanied by two stylistic alternate sets.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Ernesto as a geometric sans serif designed for the corporate identity of the Ewerk Freiburg cultural space in southern Germany. The letterforms were derived from the venue's architecture, built around 1920, a period when geometric sans serifs such as Kabel and Futura were emerging. Geometrisation and construction were the guiding themes, resulting in an uppercase-only typeface available in three weights with two stylistic alternate sets.

name: Rekja
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Rekja as a transitional typeface, with round and dynamic character balanced by strong edges and corners, rooted in hand-drawn italic forms.
summary: A transitional serif by Anton Studer designed italic-first, lending the upright a lively rhythm suited to continuous reading.
description: Designed by starting with the italic rather than the roman, Rekja carries a warmth and dynamism into its upright forms that more conventionally sequenced text faces often lack. Strong edges and corners sit alongside round strokes, giving it a distinctive tension at text sizes. The family spans two weights with italics, and the name itself means 'tracking' in Icelandic. It received a Certificate of Excellence at TDC New York, an honourable mention in Typographica's Favourite Typefaces of 2013 and a Bronze at the Joseph Binder Award in 2014.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Rekja as a transitional typeface designed by Anton Studer with a round and dynamic character marked by strong edges and corners. The italic was designed first to define the leading characteristics of the upright roman. Rekja is positioned as a literary text face optimised for legibility at small sizes, with the aim of serving the visualisation of human language. Its name means 'tracking' in Icelandic.

name: A. ABF Silhouette
classification_notes: A display typeface derived from ABF Petit, presented as negative letterforms set within solid black blocks, making it purely a display and experimental type specimen rather than a functional text face.
summary: A display typeface by Philippe Apeloig in which ABF Petit letterforms appear as white negative shapes cut from solid black blocks, designed for the ABF library conference theme of Space and Architecture.
description: Philippe Apeloig's ABF Silhouette inverts and abstracts the ABF Petit typeface, presenting each letter as a white void within a solid black rectangle. The result reads as a bold, graphic counterpart to its sibling, where the architecture of the letterform is implied by the surrounding mass rather than by the stroke itself. Conceived around the conference theme of Space and Architecture of Libraries, it sits in the territory of bespoke exhibition and identity typography rather than general use.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe ABF Silhouette as a playful combination of ABF Petit placed inside solid black blocks, functioning as a negatived and abstracted version of its sibling typeface. They note that the letters visually reference the conference theme of Space and Architecture of Libraries, making Silhouette a powerful counterpart to ABF Petit. It was designed by Philippe Apeloig, released in 2013, and received a German Design Award in 2015.

name: A. ABF Petit
classification_notes: Letterforms built from rectangles and rounded ovals to suggest architectural floor plans, making this a highly conceptual geometric display face with no conventional typographic subcategory.
summary: A conceptual display face by Philippe Apeloig, built from rectangles and rounded ovals to evoke the aerial floor plan of a library.
description: Designed for the Association des Bibliothécaires de France corporate identity, the letterforms read simultaneously as typography and as library furniture, worktables, shelves and digital monitors seen from above. The face belongs to a three-part family alongside ABF Silhouette and ABF Linéaire, each evolving with the organisation's annual themes. Its starting theme, 'Demain la bibliothèque', gives the design its core vocabulary of minimal geometric shapes. Won a German Design Award in 2015.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe ABF Petit as a typeface Philippe Apeloig created for the French Librarians Association's corporate identity, derived from the theme 'libraries of tomorrow'. The letterforms suggest an aerial view of a library floor plan, evoking worktables, shelves, chairs, monitors and tablets through a restrained palette of rectangles and rounded ovals. It is the founding typeface of the ABF family, developed alongside ABF Silhouette and ABF Linéaire.

name: Medien
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly classifies Medien as a humanistic sans serif, drawing comparisons to Frutiger and Syntax, with its structure grounded in screen pixel grids while remaining legible in print contexts.
summary: A humanist sans designed by Anton Studer at ZHdK, built on a pixel-grid structure for legibility across screen and print.
description: Developed as a Master thesis project at the Zurich University of the Arts, Medien takes its underlying structure from screen pixel grids whilst remaining unobtrusive in print. The family sits in the humanist sans tradition alongside Frutiger and Syntax, sharing their organic stroke modulation and open apertures. Three weights with matching italics make it suited to corporate communications, digital interfaces and editorial work in magazines and newspapers.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Medien as a typeface designed for any media, its structure derived from a screen pixel grid. Intended for corporate communications, online applications and printed publications, the family draws on the humanist sans serif tradition, with Frutiger and Syntax cited as points of reference. The typeface was created by Anton Studer as his Master thesis for the MAS Type Design programme at ZHdK, and received nominations and awards from the German Design Award, Granshan, the European Design Awards and TDC Tokyo.

name: A. Poudre
classification_notes: A. Poudre is a display typeface composed of out-of-focus dots arranged in a grid, with density variations creating weight differences across seven steps. Its entirely non-standard construction places it firmly in the experimental display category.
summary: Originally designed for an Alfred Nobel exhibition poster, this typeface constructs letterforms from grids of out-of-focus dots whose density shifts across seven named steps from near-empty to tightly packed.
description: Philippe Apeloig designed this face for a poster about Alfred Nobel, drawing on the inventor's work with dynamite and subatomic particles. Letters are built from large halftone-style dots on a grid; the seven steps from Zero to Six vary the density and spacing of those dots rather than changing conventional stroke weight. Within a word the shifting density creates an undulating, powder-like dispersal across the surface, allowing emphasis at the level of a letter, a syllable or a whole word.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe A. Poudre as a typeface originally created by Apeloig for a poster advertising an exhibition on inventor Alfred Nobel. The letterforms are composed of large out-of-focus dots in a grid, inspired by Nobel's dynamite and work with subatomic particles. Dot density ranges from heavy and tightly packed to smaller and widely spaced across the seven weights, producing an undulating movement and allowing emphasis at the level of individual letters, syllables or words.

name: A. Coupé
classification_notes: A highly stylised display face with geometric cuts interrupting letterforms, designed for poster use. Its mechanical aesthetic and deliberate disruptions of symmetry place it firmly in the display category with geometric underpinnings.
summary: Originally drawn by Philippe Apeloig for a Ralph Lauren car exhibition poster, this ten-weight display face uses clean geometric cuts to play with symmetry within each letterform.
description: Designed by Philippe Apeloig for a poster promoting Ralph Lauren's vintage car collection at the Musée des Arts Décoratifs in Paris, the typeface draws on the streamlined engineering of classic automobiles. Each letterform is interrupted by precise cuts that introduce a tension between mechanical rigidity and aesthetic elegance, disrupting symmetry to give each character its own character. First released as a single weight in 2013, the family expanded in 2018 to ten numbered weights and received a German Design Award.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe A. Coupé as rooted in the streamlined elegance of vintage sports cars. Philippe Apeloig originally designed the letters for a poster for an exhibition of Ralph Lauren's car collection, which made its European premiere at the Musée des Arts Décoratifs in Paris. Clean cuts interrupt parts of each letterform, playing with symmetry and asymmetry and adding personality, much like the tension between the mechanical and aesthetic natures of a sports car.

name: A. ABF Linéaire
classification_notes: A geometric sans built from linear contours derived from the rectangular and oval forms of ABF Petit, retaining strict geometry while introducing fluidity through outline-based construction.
summary: A geometric sans by Philippe Apeloig that redraws the solid forms of ABF Petit as linear outlines across six weights from Thin to Bold.
description: Designed by Philippe Apeloig and developed by Nouvelle Noire, this typeface takes the rectangular, oval and dot-based geometry of ABF Petit and strips it back to linear contours, producing characters that feel more fluid despite retaining their underlying geometric logic. Six weights from Thin to Bold give a useful range for typographic hierarchies. The family is intended to work alongside its sister fonts in the ABF series or independently, where the linear construction adds a spare, structured energy to display and editorial settings.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe ABF Linéaire as a transformation of ABF Petit's letter shapes into linear contours. The characters remain geometric but move away from solid rectangles, ovals and dots toward something more fluid. Six weights from Thin to Bold offer a broad typographic range, and the typeface is designed to work either alongside its sister fonts or independently, adding dynamism to any message it conveys. It received a German Design Award.

name: ATL Aleph
classification_notes: Aleph is a display typeface built on a calligraphic system inspired by Roger Excoffon's Banco, with disjointed strokes and open letterforms that place it firmly in the display category rather than script, as it retains upright Latin construction.
summary: A calligraphic display typeface by Philippe Apeloig, drawing on the disjointed broad-nib energy of Roger Excoffon's Banco.
description: Designed by Philippe Apeloig and released in 2013, Aleph is built on a calligraphic gesture of open, disjointed strokes that recall a broad nib pen arrested mid-movement. Vertical strokes and slight curves form letters that are never fully closed, giving the typeface both immediacy and structural resolve. The result sits between historical calligraphy and a sharp contemporary sensibility, earning a German Design Award.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe Aleph as drawn on a basic calligraphic system inspired by Roger Excoffon's Banco from 1952. Based on hand gesture, the disjointed lines recall a broad nib pen in motion, with letters of vertical strokes and slight curves that are never fully closed, as if halted mid-stroke. The minimally constructed typeface balances spontaneity with strength, offering a sharp modern look that contrasts with the historical roots of calligraphy.

name: A. Izocel
classification_notes: An entirely custom, expressive typeface that prioritises aesthetic and mood over legibility, built from thin lines and solid triangular shapes with deliberate misalignment above and below the baseline. Its origins in a single album cover commission place it firmly in the display category with no conventional subcategory applying.
summary: An experimental display typeface by Philippe Apeloig, originally drawn for a limited-edition facsimile score by composer Pascal Dusapin, combining thin strokes with solid triangular shapes that drift above and below the baseline.
description: Conceived for the cover of 'O Mensch!', a boxed portfolio containing a facsimile of Dusapin's handwritten Lieder score based on Nietzsche's poems, Apeloig built letterforms that deliberately court illegibility. Thin lines and solid triangular shapes shift rhythmically above and below the conventional top- and baselines, evoking the movement of musical notation. The typeface won a German Design Award, and its release through Nouvelle Noire marks it as a rare case of a commission-born design entering the commercial catalogue.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe A. Izocel as originating from a few letters drawn for the cover of 'O Mensch!', a limited-edition boxed portfolio housing a facsimile of composer Pascal Dusapin's handwritten score for a Lieder cycle based on Nietzsche's poems. Apeloig designed letterforms that mix thin lines with solid triangular shapes, shifting above and below the baseline like musical notes, creating a deliberately challenging aesthetic that resists easy legibility.

name: A. Ndebele
classification_notes: A purely uppercase geometric display face inspired by Ndebele house-painting traditions, using rounded angles and progressive vertical breaks rather than conventional letterform construction.
summary: A geometric uppercase display face by Philippe Apeloig, drawn from South African Ndebele house-painting traditions and structured around a progressive vertical break applied across three stylistic stages.
description: Commissioned in 2010 for the visual identity of 'Afrique Contemporaine', the journal of the Agence Française de Développement, the typeface abstracts the vivid geometric symbols of Ndebele house-painting into stylised letterforms. All characters are uppercase with rounded angles, and each letter carries a fine vertical break that widens progressively across the three variants: Plain, Half and Quarter. The result sits at the boundary between representation and abstraction, making it a tight fit for editorial display and identity work with a strong graphic character.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe A. Ndebele as originating from a 2010 commission by the Agence Française de Développement, for which Philippe Apeloig developed letterforms inspired by the South African Ndebele tribe's tradition of painting houses with vivid geometric symbols. The font uses only geometric uppercase letters with rounded angles, and features a fine vertical break in each letter that is progressively enlarged across three variants. It won a German Design Award.

name: A. Ali
classification_notes: Constructed from vertical and horizontal rectangles with soft angles, the letterforms are grid-based and highly geometric, placing this firmly in display territory with no conventional text-type characteristics.
summary: Designed by Philippe Apeloig for a book cover project, built from rectangular forms arranged on a grid as though folded into place.
description: Each letterform is composed of vertical and horizontal rectangles modulated by soft angles, giving the alphabet a schematic, architectural quality. The design originated from 'Recto-Verso', a book by artist Ali Kazma, for which Apeloig devised new letters informed by the physical dimensions and weight of the book itself. The typeface takes its name from Kazma and won a German Design Award.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describe A. Ali as letters created from vertical and horizontal rectangles with soft angles, arranged as if on a grid and recalling the schematisation of text columns. The design originated from a cover Apeloig created for 'Recto-Verso', a book by Ali Kazma, with the letterforms conceived as if in a game of origami.

name: ATL Octobre
classification_notes: Built from pixel-like squares with a low-resolution bitmap aesthetic and stencil-like missing parts, placing it firmly in the display category as an experimental, decorative face with no conventional text-use intent.
summary: A pixel-brick display face by Philippe Apeloig, born from a 1993 Villa Medici residency and released digitally in 2013.
description: Conceived by Philippe Apeloig during a residency at the Villa Medici in Rome in 1993–94, each letterform is constructed from solid square units with deliberate gaps that read as stencil cutouts, evoking early computer screens at low resolution. Originally made for the Octobre en Normandie Festival of dance and music, the typeface carries that programme's mix of classical rigour and contemporary spirit into its geometric structure. It won a German Design Award and stands as a rare example of a commissioned cultural identity becoming a fully realised retail typeface.
foundry_blurb: Nouvelle Noire describes Octobre as based on an agglomeration of pixel-like squares and a low-resolution bitmap aesthetic, with letters designed as solid bricks interrupted by missing parts suggesting stencil lettering. Apeloig created the font during his 1993–94 Villa Medici residency, naming it after the Octobre en Normandie Festival, which presents dance and music performed by world-renowned conductors and musicians.
